---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
---

<!-- the section below is shown only if rendering is beamer from latex. (and format:revealjs is commmented) -->

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{2.2 - Item Response Theory}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```
:::

## Item Response Theory

Item Response Theory is a family of more advanced methods that attempt to independently model task difficulty and subject ability.
\pause

They (often) assume **unidimensionality** of the measured construct.

## Unidimensionality
```{=latex}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        % Draw the arrow
        \draw[thick, ->] (0,0) -- (10,0);
        
        % Add ticks and numbers
        \foreach \x in {0,2,4,6,8} {
            \draw[thick] (\x,0.2) -- (\x,-0.2); % Tick mark
            \node[below] at (\x,-0.2) {\x}; % Number
        }
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center} 
(\emph{es. My result is dependend by an ability/constructs that may be rapresented along a single dimension})
```


## Item Response Theory

Example: Rasch Model (or 1-PL model)
\vspace{2em}

```{=latex}
\small
\begin{align}
  \Pr(X_{ni}=1 \mid \theta_n, \beta_i)
  &= \frac{\exp(\theta_n - \beta_i)}{1 + \exp(\theta_n - \beta_i)}
  \label{eq:rasch_prob}
  %\\
  %\Pr(X_{ni}=0 \mid \theta_n, \beta_i) &= \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\theta_n - \beta_i)}
  %\label{eq:rasch_prob0}
\end{align}

$\theta$ = ability
$\beta$ = difficulty parameter
```

\vspace{2em}

[[https://www.publichealth.columbia.edu/research/population-health-methods/item-response-theory]{.underline}](https://www.publichealth.columbia.edu/research/population-health-methods/item-response-theory)


## Item Response Theory – Rasch models

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{\footnotesize Dichotomous Item Response Data for 10 Participants and 6 Items}
\label{tab:rasch_data}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\hline
\textbf{Participant} & \textbf{i1} & \textbf{i2} & \textbf{i3} & \textbf{i4} & \textbf{i5} & \textbf{i6} & \textbf{Total} \\
\hline
p1  & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 6 \\
p2  & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 4 \\  % missing i2, unexpected fail
p3  & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 4 \\  % missing i3, unexpected fail
p4  & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\  % missing i1, unexpected fail
p5  & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\
p6  & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\  % unexpected pass on i6
p7  & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
p8  & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
p9  & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
p10 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\  % unexpected pass on i3
\hline
\textbf{Total} & 6 & 7 & 6 & 5 & 3 & 2 & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}
```

<!--
\begin{figure}
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{Figures/Rasch_matrix.png}
\end{figure}
-->


## Item Response Theory – Validity and Reliability

Also tests developed with IRT have associated severe properties, including some related to **Validity** and **Reliability** as in CTT (although not necessarily with these labels).


## Classical Test Theory vs Item Response Theory

*Why relatively few tests are based on IRT, despite its methodological rigor?*
